channel.title: Home on Mike Wilcome’s Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Home on Mike Wilcome’s Blog

item.title: Finding Motivation in Tech
item.description:
A Journey of Building and Serving

I wanted to talk a little bit about getting things done. For a long time, I’ve struggled—and seen others struggle—with trying to actually be motivated to do things. It might be small daily tasks, like taking out those plastic cups lying around the room, or larger goals, like tackling side projects for work.

The problem is that in tech, we have to constantly learn and move forward. If we don’t, we get left behind by the next person who’s motivated to learn and move up. I’ve recently found that my motivation is tied, in part, to my interest in something, but it can also be tied to my desire for a better quality of life. I often think, “If I can build something that people will use, not only could I make some money, but it would benefit people as well.”

item.title: Mandelbrot Spiral Project
item.description:
Mandelbrot Spiral

I built a project called mandelbrot-spiral. This was a “lunch break” project today. It’s a visualization of the Mandelbrot set that zooms in fast with a spiral twist. Wrote it quickly with Grok (xAI) helping in JavaScript using p5.js. The idea came from messing around with the Mandelbrot set—wanted to see it move, not just sit there. Took some trial and error to get the zoom right, but it’s out on GitHub now: mwilcome/mandelbrot-spiral. Here’s what it’s about.

item.title: Creating an Impossible Gear Illusion with p5.js
item.description:
A Wild Idea Comes to Life

I recently teamed up with Grok, an AI from xAI, to tackle a fun challenge: building an optical illusion that makes you question how gears work. The result is the Impossible Gear Cascade—a stack of gears where the bottom one spins the same way as the top, which shouldn’t happen in real life. We brought it to life using p5.js, a JavaScript tool that’s perfect for creative projects like this.

item.title: Building Backend Pipelines for Comporium: Taming the Sales Beast
item.description:
Diving into Comporium’s Telephone/Internet/Security Sales

Back at Orange Bees, I worked with Comporium, a Southeast company who work on phone, internet, TV, and security services across the Carolinas. I was tasked with building and supporting a fleet of Java microservices to power their sales pipelines—an internal tool that sales reps lean on to wrestle orders into shape. It was about streamlining a tangled mess into something sleek, all while juggling the ins and outs of their massive product lineup.

item.title: Supporting Michelin FixPix Backend: Roadside Rescue Done Right
item.description:
Hitting the Road with Michelin

From August 2022 to December 2023, I got my hands dirty at Orange Bees, powering up the backend for Michelin FixPix—a slick little app tied into Michelin’s OnCall roadside assistance gig. This was a challenge that had me wrestling with Java, Apache Camel, Vaadin, Velocity, and Spring to help their roadside services more efficient.

The Mission: Keeping Techs Rolling

Michelin’s OnCall system is all about getting stranded drivers back on the road, fast—24/7, coast-to-coast, no excuses. FixPix fits right in, giving roadside techs real-time tools to handle breakdowns in the middle of nowhere. I was the guy behind the scenes, building a backend that delivered locations, data points, and quick comms to those techs in rural spots where cell service is dicey. It took some serious technical know-how to untangle the mess of requirements and lead the charge—think of it like threading a needle while the truck’s still rolling.

item.title: Sync My Health Data: Wearables for Veterans
item.description:
Turning Wearables into Lifelines

From April 2019 to August 2022, I stepped up as the lead architect for Sync My Health Data—a beast of a project that’s honestly one of my proudest wins. Picture this: I built a backend API that ties together 20+ health apps and 800+ devices, pulling live data straight to VA doctors. We’re talking heavy hitters like Fitbit and Garmin, plus a slew of others, all feeding into a system I crafted with Java and Server-Sent Events (SSE). The mission? Deliver real-time insights on heart health, diabetes, and fitness for veterans—data that’s making a difference every day.

item.title: Rebuilding VBMS Correspondence Web Service
item.description:
Project Start

Back at Booz Allen Hamilton, from September 2015 to March 2019, I took on a large project: the VBMS Correspondence Web Service. When I started, it was a mess—20+ modules tangled up in a large monolithic app, part of an even bigger system for the Veterans Affairs. I had to rebuild it, and make it work for vets needing their benefits. Over the years, I led the charge to split it out into its own service, rewriting it as a lean Jersey and Spring Boot microservice, with a sprinkle of Groovy for good measure. This resulted in a streamlined system that churned out formatted PDF documents from requests, cutting through the VA’s correspondence mess.

item.title: About Me
item.description:
Mike Wilcome

Principal Engineer | Full-Stack Java Expert | Tech Leader

I’m Mike Wilcome, a Principal Engineer passionate about building innovative software solutions. With expertise in full-stack Java development, microservices, and leadership, I’ve spent over a decade delivering impactful projects in tech, healthcare, and veteran services.

Education

Bachelor of Science in Computer Science
Charleston Southern University
2009 - 2014
I learned the foundations of software engineering while honing skills in leadership and service. This degree focused on alot of the deeper computer science concepts and math C++, advanced data structures, system architecture, calc 3, physics 2+, linear algebra, etc. I was also tailored to Java so I could focus on dev work post-college.
